title: [aerodactyl] Battle Guide
summary: A 130 Speed [rock]/[flying] lead that guarantees [stealth-rock] and [taunt] both go up on turn one, denying opposing setup plans.
strengths: 130 Speed wins essentially every lead race in the format
[stealth-rock] plus [taunt] shuts down opposing lead setups
weaknesses: 80/65/75 bulk folds to fast special attackers
Multiple weaknesses to Ice, Electric, [rock], Steel, and Water
Only 105 Attack — damage output is lead-tier, not breaker-tier

recommendedMoves.reason: Lead-defining hazard setup for team pressure
Shuts down opposing hazard and setup leads completely
Coverage for Steel and Electric walls

recommendedItems.reason: Guarantees the turn so always goes up
Boosts [flying] STAB on the offensive build

bodyMarkdown:
## Overview
[aerodactyl] is a [rock]/[flying] lead whose entire design is built around winning the opening turn of the game. 130 base Speed sits near the top of the format, which means [aerodactyl] goes first in almost every lead mirror, and from there the plan is simple. [aerodactyl] isn't a breaker or a cleaner, but the tempo it seizes on turn one routinely snowballs into a won mid-game.

## Strengths
The Speed tier is the single most important strength. Very few unboosted Pokemon outpace 130 base, so [aerodactyl] reliably moves first in the all-important opening turn. [taunt] removes the threat of opposing hazard leads, status spreaders, and defog users — the most common turn-one responses [aerodactyl] would otherwise face. [unnerve] is a minor but nonzero bonus that disables the opposing Pokemon's berry trigger while [aerodactyl] is on the field.

## Weaknesses & Answers
[aerodactyl]'s 80/65/75 bulk is thin enough that any fast special attacker removes it before the second turn. It's weak to Ice, Electric, [rock], Steel, and Water, which means the natural answer pool is large. Damage output is only 105 Attack, which means [aerodactyl] can't muscle through walls and has to rely on its utility moves.

## Recommended Moveset
[stealth-rock] is the locked utility slot because that's the reason [aerodactyl] exists in the lineup. [taunt] is mandatory to prevent the opposing lead from setting its own hazards or status spreading, and the order of those two moves is usually [taunt] first, rocks second. [stone-edge] is the [rock] STAB finisher for when [aerodactyl] needs to damage something, and [earthquake] covers Steel and Electric pivots that try to absorb the [rock] move.

## Item · Ability · Tera
[focus-sash] is the standard lead item. [sharp-beak] is an alternate choice for the rare offensive build that wants brave-bird or acrobatics as the [flying] STAB instead of the utility plan. The ability should be [unnerve] for the berry shutdown; [rock]-head and pressure are narrow alternatives. Tera [rock] maxes the [stone-edge] damage, while Tera [flying] erases the Ground weakness and sets up a cleaner follow-up.

## Teammates & Synergy
The whole point of leading [aerodactyl] is to build tempo for a follow-up breaker. Hazard synergy is valuable: a second hazard setter (spikes or toxic-spikes) compounds the chip damage that [stealth-rock] starts. A fast pivot lets [aerodactyl] retreat safely after its lead turn, and a special wall on the back line covers the biggest defensive weakness left behind.

## Play Patterns
Turn one [aerodactyl]'s play is almost scripted. Turn two use whichever move wasn't used on turn one, then pivot out before special attackers remove it. [stone-edge] and [earthquake] are finishers for weakened opponents later in the match when [aerodactyl] enters as a cleanup pivot. Save the Tera for a specific defensive swing you can't survive otherwise.
